sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 13, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Player (green, 2x3) at rows 41-43, cols 32-35 - Three blue squares (2x2 each) in dark gray frames (3x3 each) arranged in a triangle - Blue square 3 frame blocks upward path (rows 36-39, cols 32-35) - UP movement moves player up 4 rows when clear; blocked when obstacle in path - Goal unclear - likely navigate to collect/interact with blue squares
- Plan: Navigate to remaining blue squares and press SPACE to remove their frames.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 12 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After SPACE, something different happened:
- The left dark frame is STILL there at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
- But a new blue square appeared at rows 33-34, cols 21-22 (next to the player!)
- The player is still at rows 32-35, cols 16-18
So SPACE didn't remove the dark frame this time. Instead, it seems to have "spawned" a blue square near the player. This is different from the first SPACE press. Let me reconsider the mechanic.
Wait, let me look more carefully. Before SPACE:
- Player was at rows 32-35, cols 16-18
- Left dark frame at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
After SPACE:
- Player still at rows 32-35, cols 16-18
- Left dark frame still at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
- New blue square at rows 33-34, cols 21-22
Hmm, the blue square appeared to the right of the player. Maybe SPACE creates a copy of the blue square from the adjacent frame? Or maybe SPACE does something different depending on position.
Actually wait - looking at the image, the player is now next to a blue square. Maybe the mechanic is that SPACE "extracts" or "copies" the blue square from the frame? But the frame is still there...
Let me think about this differently. Maybe SPACE doesn't remove frames at all. The first time I pressed SPACE, the dark frame around blue square 3 disappeared. But this time, the frame is still there. What's different?
First SPACE: Player was at rows 36-39, cols 28-30, adjacent to the dark frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-35. The frame was removed.
Second SPACE: Player is at rows 32-35, cols 16-18, adjacent to the dark frame at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. The frame was NOT removed, but a blue square appeared.
Hmm, the difference might be in the relative positioning. In the first case, the player was to the LEFT of the frame. In the second case, the player is BELOW the frame.
Actually, looking more carefully at the first case:
- Player: rows 36-39, cols 28-30
- Frame: rows 36-39, cols 32-35
They were side by side (same row range).
Second case:
- Player: rows 32-35, cols 16-18
- Frame: rows 28-31, cols 16-19
They overlap in columns but the player is below.
Maybe SPACE only works when the player is side-by-side with the frame? Or maybe there's a specific alignment needed.
Actually, I notice the new blue square is at rows 33-34, cols 21-22. This is to the right of the player (cols 16-18). Maybe SPACE creates a blue square in the direction the player is facing or something.
Let me try a different approach. Let me go to the top-right blue square and try to remove its frame. The top-right frame is at rows 24-27, cols 44-47.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated:
- SPACE behavior is inconsistent - first time removed a frame, second time spawned a blue square
- Player at rows 32-35, cols 16-18
- One dark frame removed (bottom-center), two remain
- New blue square appeared at rows 33-34, cols 21-22 after second SPACE
- Need to understand SPACE mechanics better - may depend on relative position

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the adjacency to understand the mechanic
seg = current_frame.segmentation
adj = seg['adjacency_list']

# Find player and dark frame ids
player_id = None
frame_ids = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        player_id = n['id']
        print(f"Player id={player_id}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        frame_ids.append(n['id'])
        print(f"Dark frame id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check adjacency
print(f"\nAdjacency list:")
for a in adj:
    if player_id in a or any(f in a for f in frame_ids):
        print(f"  {a}")

# The top-right frame is at rows 24-27, cols 44-47
# Player is at rows 32-35, cols 16-18
# Need to navigate there - go RIGHT and UP
# Try moving RIGHT to get to the top-right frame
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"\nAfter RIGHT, Player: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
